title: Presidential communication: advanced
description: Practice your skills in this quiz on how communication technology has changed the president's relationship with the national constituency and the other branches.
topic_path: How does the president communicate with the public?

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following explains how modern technology has changed the president’s ability to respond to political issues?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It has made it easier for the president to rapidly respond to political issues by appealing directly to the public
It has made the presidency less transparent because the president’s communications director monitors all responses to political issues
It has limited the president’s power to connect with the other branches before responding to political issues
It has changed the relationship between the president and the media, allowing the president to monitor stories before they are released to the public

assessment_items.question_content:
|| **Percent watching or listening to Bush post-9/11 speech on terrorism** ||
**Age** | **Percentage (%)**
- | - 
**18-34** | 67
**35-39** | 82
**60+** | 86

Source: ABC/*Washington Post* poll, September 20, 2001

**Which of the following statements is reflected in the data in the table above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Technology such as broadcast media gives the president an avenue to rapidly respond to a majority of American citizens about political issues
Presidents today are less likely to use modern technology such as broadcast media to respond to political issues because few American citizens watch or listen
People under the age of 35 are more likely than those 35 or over to use broadcast media to watch or listen to a president’s response to a political issue
People aged 60 or over are less likely than those under 60 to use broadcast media to watch or listen to a president’s response to a political issue

assessment_items.question_content:
>“In addition to educating the country, [President Barack Obama’s] goal is to frame the political debate and set an agenda that the next Democratic presidential nominee can run on next year—an agenda that Obama hopes will put opposition Republicans at a big disadvantage.

>Obama has been getting lots of attention for bypassing the Republican-controlled Congress and using his unilateral powers, such as executive orders, to get his way. But his power to persuade is another vital weapon in the president's arsenal, since he can command media and public attention almost whenever he wants.”

>-Kenneth T. Walsh, [U.S. News & World Report](https://www.usnews.com/news/the-report/articles/2015/07/17/obamas-use-of-the-bully-pulpit-will-determine-his-legacy), 2015

**Which of the following statements best summarizes the author’s argument?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The president’s ability to command media attention gives him an advantage in shaping the national policy agenda
The president is exceeding his constitutional authority by bypassing Congress and appealing directly to the public
Media appearances are an ineffective way to educate the country about important policy debates
The president’s popularity depends upon his ability to craft a charismatic persona in national media appearances

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements explains how social media has enhanced the power of the presidency?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It gives the president a direct line of communication to the national constituency to influence national policy
It gives the president the power to accurately measure public opinion on the presidency
It gives the president the ability to nationally broadcast the State of the Union
It gives the president a direct line to the media in an attempt to control the stories they release

assessment_items.question_content:
>![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2318ac35771a84005c7eaab50195fce7.png)
>Source: Twitter 

**Which of the following explains how President Obama is using social media to affect policy in the example above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To inform American citizens of his policy goals
To discourage the American public from supporting congressional policy goals
To appeal to the public for reelection
To persuade the American public to buy more diapers

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Amid this much-changed communication landscape, the lingering question of whether the untapped wisdom of the public should be harnessed in governmental decisions suddenly commanded a fresh look, as social media increased the capability of governments worldwide to more fully involve ordinary citizens in policymaking.”

>-M. Barris, A. Jain, and J. Katz, *The Social Media President: Barack Obama and the Politics of Political Engagement,* 2013

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the authors most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Social media has changed the president’s relationship with the national constituency by involving more citizens in policymaking decisions
Social media has changed the president’s relationship to Congress by involving more members of Congress in policymaking decisions
Social media has had no effect on the president’s relationship to the national constituency or Congress
Social media has changed the president’s relationship to the national constituency by involving fewer citizens in policymaking decisions

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how a president would use a bully pulpit to achieve policy goals?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: By appealing directly to the public, the president hopes members of the public will influence their representatives to support those policy goals
By appealing directly to Congress, the president hopes Congress will create legislation that supports those policy goals
By appealing directly to the states, the president hopes the states will pass legislation that supports those policy goals
By appealing directly to the Supreme Court, the president hopes the Supreme Court will rule conflicting legislation as unconstitutional

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios is an example of the president using the bully pulpit approach to achieve policy goals?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The president appeals to the American public to contact their representatives and urge them to support increased military spending
The president meets with senators from the opposing party to encourage them to support a piece of legislation that would increase funding of the arts
The president publishes a memoir after leaving office, detailing her decisions on major events that happened while she was in office
The president appears on television to debate a presidential nominee on his record of policy accomplishments while in office

assessment_items.question_content:
>“In addition to educating the country, [President Barack Obama’s] goal is to frame the political debate and set an agenda that the next Democratic presidential nominee can run on next year—an agenda that Obama hopes will put opposition Republicans at a big disadvantage.

>Obama has been getting lots of attention for bypassing the Republican-controlled Congress and using his unilateral powers, such as executive orders, to get his way. But his power to persuade is another vital weapon in the president's arsenal, since he can command media and public attention almost whenever he wants.”

>-Kenneth T. Walsh, [U.S. News & World Report](https://www.usnews.com/news/the-report/articles/2015/07/17/obamas-use-of-the-bully-pulpit-will-determine-his-legacy), 2015

**The strategy described in the passage above is an example of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The bully pulpit
Cloture
Formal powers
Checks and balances

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the graphs and answer the question below. 

*Presidential approval ratings over time, based on sample responses to the question “Do you approve or disapprove of the way [the president] is handling his job as president?”*

**Harry Truman (1945-1952)**
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/df750dd4adc423230819072012d76467.png)
Source: [Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Gallup_Poll-Approval_Rating-Harry_S_Truman.png)

**George W. Bush (2001-2009)**
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f18b63ac6f33ba4561411cc3d6e71fd2.png)
Source: [Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Gallup_Poll-Approval_Rating-George_W_Bush.png)

**Which of the following is an accurate conclusion based on a comparison of the trends in the line graphs?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The increased speed of communication has led to more rapid changes in public perception of the president
Presidential approval ratings have become more difficult to measure accurately due to increased communication speeds.
Presidential approval ratings have increased over time as the public gains greater exposure to the day-to-day workings of the presidency
Presidential approval ratings are typically lower during wartime crises

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the graph and answer the question below. 

![Graph showing the average State of the Union address length in minutes of each US president since Richard Nixon. The address lengths are as follows: 
Richard Nixon   35
Gerald Ford	46
Jimmy Carter 	37
Ronald Reagan	40
George H.W. Bush	45
Bill Clinton	74
George W. Bush	52
Barack Obama	62
Donald Trump	80](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0a8171379d90b688878a35dea3df632a.png)
Source: [The American Presidency Project](http://www.presidency.ucsb.edu/sou_minutes.php)

**Which of the following best describes a trend in the line graph above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: State of the Union addresses have become longer over time
State of the Union addresses by Republican presidents have been longer than those of Democratic presidents
Televised State of the Union addresses tend to last longer than those broadcast on the radio
State of the Union addresses never last longer than an hour

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the graph and answer the question below. 

![Graph showing the average State of the Union address length in minutes of each US president since Richard Nixon. The address lengths are as follows: 
Richard Nixon   35
Gerald Ford	46
Jimmy Carter 	37
Ronald Reagan	40
George H.W. Bush	45
Bill Clinton	74
George W. Bush	52
Barack Obama	62
Donald Trump	80](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0a8171379d90b688878a35dea3df632a.png)
Source: [The American Presidency Project](http://www.presidency.ucsb.edu/sou_minutes.php)

**Which of the following is an accurate conclusion based on a comparison of the trends in the line graph and your knowledge of presidential communication strategies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Presidents have increased the length of their State of the Union messages to make the most of the opportunity to influence the electorate directly
Presidents have decreased the length of their State of the Union messages to accommodate shrinking attention spans
Presidents have increased the length of their State of the Union messages to limit the available media time for opposition responses
Presidents have turned toward social media as the major means of communicating with the electorate directly
